explanation: This idiom means joking or teasing someone playfully, not being serious.
usage:
Pattern: pulling your leg
  • Relax, I’m pulling your leg.
  • He was pulling my leg about the surprise.

explanation: To crack someone up means to make them laugh a lot. It is common in casual conversation.
usage:
Pattern: crack me up / made me laugh
  • Your story cracked me up.
  • That was hilarious.
spanish: Ese chiste me hizo reír mucho.

text: I’m {{c1::on cloud nine}} after hearing the good news.
explanation: This idiom describes being extremely happy or excited.
usage:
Pattern: on cloud nine
  • She was on cloud nine after the promotion.
  • We were on cloud nine all weekend.
spanish: Estoy en la cima de la felicidad después de escuchar la buena noticia.

explanation: This idiom means that something is not very difficult. It is often said humorously.
usage:
Pattern: not rocket science
  • It’s not rocket science; just follow the steps.
  • This part is not rocket science.

text: We’re all in the {{c1::same boat}} here.
explanation: This idiom means that everyone is dealing with the same situation or problem.
usage:
Pattern: in the same boat
  • We’re all in the same boat with this deadline.
  • Everyone was in the same boat after the outage.
spanish: Todos estamos en la misma situación.

explanation: This idiom refers to the final problem that makes someone lose patience or stop tolerating something.
usage:
Pattern: the last straw
  • Missing the deadline was the last straw.
  • That was the last straw for me.
spanish: Eso fue lo que terminó de colmar el vaso.

scenario: Idioms: A Lot on My Plate 🍽️
text: I’ve got a lot on my {{c1::plate}} right now.
explanation: This idiom means someone has many responsibilities or tasks to manage.
usage:
Pattern: have a lot on your plate
  • I have a lot on my plate this week.
  • She’s got too much on her plate.
spanish: Tengo muchas cosas que atender en este momento.

scenario: Idioms: You Can Say That Again 🙌
explanation: This phrase means that someone strongly agrees with what was just said.
usage:
Pattern: you can say that again
  • You can say that again.
  • I completely agree — you can say that again.
spanish: Eso es exactamente lo que pienso.
